perturbation.original_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary or permanent injunction (without the necessity of showing any actual damage) or a decree of specific performance and no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a permanent injunction only, and a bond or other security shall be required in that connection.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the available relief to only a permanent injunction and requiring a bond, the modified text contradicts the possibility of obtaining a temporary restraining order or preliminary injunction without a bond, which might be permissible under Indiana law under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure, Rule 65(C)
perturbation.law_citation: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure, Rule 65(C)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65(C) of the Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure addresses security requirements for injunctions and restraining orders, stating the court may waive the bond requirement under specific conditions. The changed text mandates a bond in all cases, conflicting with the court's discretionary power under Indiana law to waive the bond.
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text allows for a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary injunction without a bond. The changed text limits the remedy to a permanent injunction only and mandates a bond, which contradicts the possibility of obtaining a temporary restraining order or preliminary injunction without a bond, potentially conflicting with Indiana law regarding waiving bond requirements. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a contract sets a requirement that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of five (5) years immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Indiana law, particularly given the specific context of the banking industry and the employee's role.  Indiana courts scrutinize non-compete agreements to ensure they are reasonably tailored to protect legitimate business interests without unduly restricting an employee's ability to earn a living.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code § 24-1-71-2 codifies standards related to non-compete agreements in certain industries. While this section does not directly forbid non-solicitation agreements, it sets a precedent of the state legislature paying close attention to these types of agreements. By extending the restriction to five years, the agreement becomes potentially unreasonable and thus unenforceable as interpreted by Indiana courts under common law, as it could be considered an undue restraint of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a one-year restriction period, which is then extended to five years in the changed contract. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that this extension could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Indiana law, potentially conflicting with common law principles regarding restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 suggests the Indiana General Assembly is actively involved in legislation, hinting at increased regulation of such agreements. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating the principle of reasonable restraint of trade, making the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of two (2) years immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: c. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Employee to terminate his or her employment with a Banking Organization, or offer or provide employment (whether such employment is with Employee or any other Person) either on a full-time or part-time, consulting or Independent Contractor basis; or d. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Independent Contractors or Agents to terminate their contract or relationship with a Banking Organization or discontinue providing services to a Banking Organization and/or Customers; or e. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any suppliers, vendors or others who were supplying services or goods to a Banking Organization during the one year period prior to Employee’s termination, to terminate, reduce, limit or change their business or relationship with a Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of ten (10) years immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: c. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Employee to terminate his or her employment with a Banking Organization, or offer or provide employment (whether such employment is with Employee or any other Person) either on a full-time or part-time, consulting or Independent Contractor basis; or d. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any Independent Contractors or Agents to terminate their contract or relationship with a Banking Organization or discontinue providing services to a Banking Organization and/or Customers; or e. Solicit, entice or persuade (or attempt to Solicit, entice or persuade) any suppliers, vendors or others who were supplying services or goods to a Banking Organization during the one year period prior to Employee’s termination, to terminate, reduce, limit or change their business or relationship with a Banking Organization.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the employee non-solicitation period to ten years is highly likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Indiana law. Indiana courts require non-compete and non-solicitation agreements to be reasonably tailored to protect the employer's legitimate business interests and not unduly restrict the employee's ability to earn a living. A ten-year period is presumptively excessive in most employment contexts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Indiana Code § 24-1-71-2 does not directly address employee non-solicitation agreements, it reflects the state's concern with the reasonableness of restrictive covenants. A ten-year non-solicitation clause would almost certainly be seen by Indiana courts as an unreasonable restraint of trade, excessively limiting the employee's future employment opportunities and therefore against public policy. The statute signals the type of arrangement that Indiana holds to a reasonableness standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the non-solicitation period from two to ten years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this is unreasonable under Indiana law, which values reasonableness in restrictive covenants. Indiana Code § 24-1-71-2 reflects this concern. A ten-year period is likely an unreasonable restraint of trade, excessively limiting employment opportunities.

perturbation.original_text: Employee acknowledges that a Banking Organization is entitled to the full post-termination restrictions on the activities set forth in Sections 5 and 6 , as applicable. Therefore, in the event any of the provisions of these Sections are breached by Employee, the commencement of the applicable post-termination restriction will not begin until Employee is in full compliance with Section 5 and/or 6 .
perturbation.changed_text: Employee acknowledges that a Banking Organization is entitled to the full post-termination restrictions on the activities set forth in Sections 5 and 6 , as applicable. Therefore, in the event any of the provisions of these Sections are breached by Employee, the Banking Organization has the right to extend the period of restriction indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Giving the Banking Organization the right to extend the period of restriction indefinitely would be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Indiana law, as it lacks a definite and reasonable time limit. Indiana courts scrutinize non-compete agreements to ensure they are reasonably tailored to protect legitimate business interests without unduly restricting an employee's ability to earn a living. The clause is not objectively reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Indiana Code § 24-1-71-2 does not directly speak to indefinite extensions, Indiana courts are generally opposed to indefinite restraint of trade. The provision would be deemed an unreasonable restraint because it lacks any temporal restriction; This violates the spirit of Indiana law that requires reasonable restrictions on employment post-termination. This statute is evidence that the legislature would frown upon indefinite extensions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces an indefinite restriction period, which the Law Explanation suggests is against the spirit of Indiana law regarding reasonable restraints on employment post-termination. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, the Law Explanation infers that indefinite extensions are not favored in Indiana law. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces this by stating the change lacks a definite and reasonable time limit.
